company.name: ResMed
company.description: ResMed makes connected devices, masks, and software for sleep apnea, COPD, and out-of-hospital care.

overview.title: Business mix
paragraphs: ResMed's core business is Sleep and Breathing Health: connected CPAP, APAP, bilevel and ventilation devices, masks, accessories, and related digital support workflows for sleep apnea and respiratory care.
The company also reports Residential Care Software, which serves home medical equipment, home health, hospice, skilled nursing, senior living, and related out-of-hospital care settings.

overview.title: Registry posture
paragraphs: This refresh treats ResMed as a regulated connected-device and consumables incumbent. Its moat comes from clinical trust, prescription and reimbursement workflows, device reliability, masks and accessories, software integration, global distribution, and intellectual property.

The most credible decentralization pressure is not a casual clone of a medical device. It is patient-owned therapy data, open analysis tools, interoperable PAP records, custom-fit mask production, and certified local manufacturing or repair networks that reduce dependence on one proprietary device-and-software stack.

moatNarrative: ResMed has a strong moat because PAP therapy sits inside medical-device regulation, clinical referral pathways, durable medical equipment distribution, payer reimbursement, patient support, and replacement-supply workflows. Its 2025 annual report also describes a large patent and design portfolio, intense but specialized competition, and revenue growth across devices, masks, and software.

The moat is not as absolute as an implantable-device moat. CPAP machines and masks are external devices, and software/data layers can be opened more easily than sterile implants. Still, validated pressure delivery, comfort, safety, warranties, service, data continuity, and clinician confidence make direct substitution materially harder than generic electronics or consumer wearables.
decentralizationNarrative: ResMed is moderately resistant to decentralization. Therapy analytics, patient data custody, device-export formats, mask fitting, replacement-parts knowledge, and noncritical accessories are plausible open or cooperative pressure points.

The pressure is weakest at the fully regulated device layer. An open CPAP reference design or custom mask network would need quality systems, traceable materials, testing, clinician oversight, and regulatory compliance before it could responsibly compete with mainstream ResMed therapy products.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 276-300; refreshed for publication on 2026-06-27 using ResMed's 2025 10-K, Q3 FY26 results, and June 2026 market-data pages.

moat.rationale: Regulated connected medical devices, clinical and reimbursement workflows, software integration, masks and accessories, global distribution, customer support, and a large patent/design portfolio create a durable but not impregnable moat.

decentralizability.rationale: Data access, analytics, custom mask fitting, accessories, repair knowledge, and certified local production can decentralize, but pressure-generating devices and patient-facing masks remain constrained by regulation, quality systems, testing, liability, and clinician trust.

profitability.rationale: ResMed reported strong Q3 FY26 profitability, including 62.2% GAAP gross margin, $499.8 million of quarterly income from operations, and $1.1399 billion of GAAP net income for the first nine months of FY26.

peRatio.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported ResMed's trailing P/E ratio at about 19.1 as of June 2026; this is a point-in-time market input and may move materially with price and earnings updates.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported ResMed market capitalization at $28.80 billion on June 25, 2026, with the page headline rounded near $29 billion during June 2026.

products.name: AirSense 11
products.category: Connected CPAP and APAP therapy device
products.summary: AirSense 11 is ResMed's connected CPAP and APAP device family for sleep apnea therapy, paired with myAir support and digital coaching features.
products.whyItMatters: AirSense 11 is the clearest expression of ResMed's device-and-software moat: pressure delivery hardware, adherence data, patient coaching, clinician workflows, and replacement accessories reinforce each other.
replacementSketch: A realistic replacement path starts with open PAP data access and patient-controlled therapy records, not an informal medical-device clone. Open analysis tools can make high-resolution sleep therapy data portable across clinicians, researchers, and devices.
A deeper replacement path would require open reference PAP hardware, validated firmware, serviceable components, and certified manufacturers that can satisfy quality and regulatory expectations.

alternatives.name: OSCAR
alternatives.summary: OSCAR is free, GPL-licensed software for viewing and analyzing high-resolution data from CPAP and related sleep apnea therapy machines.

openness.rationale: OSCAR publishes free downloads, identifies itself as open-source CPAP analysis software, links source code, and states GPLv3 licensing.

decentralizationFit.rationale: OSCAR moves therapy-data interpretation out of a single manufacturer app and supports comparison across machine types, but it still depends on commercial PAP devices and accessible data exports.

readiness.rationale: OSCAR has an active 2026 release and installers for major desktop platforms, making it usable today for technically capable patients and clinicians, though it is not a regulated hardware replacement.

costLeverage.rationale: Free data analysis can reduce software lock-in and improve self-management, but it does not replace the cost of CPAP machines, masks, supplies, clinician support, or payer workflows.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated PAP data commons
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federated PAP data commons would let patients and clinicians store therapy sessions, adherence records, leak data, pressure data, comfort notes, and outcomes in user-controlled or clinic-controlled repositories instead of relying solely on manufacturer software.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept weakens ResMed's software and data moat by separating therapy data custody and analytics from the device vendor, making PAP devices compete more on comfort, reliability, and interoperability.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Federation is the central mechanism. Bitcoin is not necessary because the problem is medical data governance and interoperability, not settlement; the useful decentralized primitive is independently operated, schema-compatible records with portable permissions.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Patients authorize data exports, clinicians review permissioned records, open-source maintainers maintain parsers and schemas, researchers query de-identified datasets, and device vendors can publish compatibility profiles.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Trust would come from device metadata, import checksums, timestamped logs, parser versioning, clinician attestations where available, reproducible analytics, and privacy-preserving deduplication. It cannot fully prove sensor or pressure accuracy without the regulated device chain.
failureModes: Vendor format changes or cloud restrictions can break imports.
Sensitive health data can be exposed if self-hosting or federation permissions are poorly managed.
Clinicians and payers may require regulated software before relying on open reports in mainstream workflows.
adoptionPath: Start with OSCAR users exporting standardized reports and de-identified compatibility data from existing PAP machines.
Add clinician pilots, permissioned patient portals, and procurement expectations that reward durable local export and interoperable therapy records.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept directly decentralizes data custody, analysis, and cross-device comparability while leaving medical-device manufacturing intact.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: OSCAR already demonstrates patient and clinician use of open PAP data analysis, but durable federation governance and clinical acceptance would require more structure.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The software primitives are feasible today, while privacy operations, stable device exports, and clinical workflow integration are the hard parts.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Open records could reduce data and app lock-in, but ResMed's device, mask, service, and reimbursement advantages would remain significant.

disruptionConcepts.name: Certified open PAP reference platform
disruptionConcepts.summary: A certified open PAP reference platform would publish hardware designs, firmware interfaces, test methods, service manuals, and quality documentation for a repairable CPAP/APAP device manufactured by qualified regional producers.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: If open PAP hardware could cross the validation and regulatory gap, ResMed's device economics would face pressure from transparent bills of materials, repairable assemblies, and interoperable firmware.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is open hardware and certified distributed manufacturing. Bitcoin is not central; the critical trust problem is pressure accuracy, reliability, traceability, service, and medical-device quality.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Clinical engineers, sleep clinicians, open-hardware maintainers, test labs, and ISO 13485-aligned manufacturers would coordinate around a shared device master record, validation data, and approved production lots.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Cheating would be constrained by signed design releases, calibration records, pressure-delivery testing, component traceability, cybersecurity review, quality-system audits, and post-market incident reporting. Open design files alone would not be enough.
failureModes: Pressure delivery, humidification, firmware, alarms, connectivity, and long-term durability may be harder to validate than the bill of materials suggests.
Regulatory approval, insurance coverage, liability, and service obligations may cost more than the electronics.
ResMed and other incumbents may retain an advantage through masks, distribution, support, and clinician familiarity.
adoptionPath: Begin with open bench test fixtures, service documentation, and nonclinical PAP research prototypes.
Move toward regulated nonprofit or cooperative manufacturing only after quality-system documentation, independent testing, and clinician-supervised pilots exist.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Open designs, public documentation, and distributed qualified production are strongly aligned with decentralization, even though medical quality controls remain centralized constraints.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open hardware communities can coordinate designs, but sleep-therapy device validation requires clinicians, regulators, manufacturers, and service operators.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The open-hardware documentation pattern is known, but a safe, approved PAP device requires pressure-control validation, quality systems, software assurance, and post-market support.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept could pressure price and repair narratives, but near-term displacement is limited by regulation, support, distribution, and clinical trust.

products.name: AirFit masks
products.summary: AirFit is ResMed's family of CPAP mask products spanning full-face, nasal, nasal-pillow, under-the-nose, and tube-up designs.
products.whyItMatters: Masks are the recurring patient interface for PAP therapy; fit, comfort, leak control, replacement parts, and habit formation can preserve the device ecosystem long after the machine is purchased.
replacementSketch: A credible replacement path starts with open fit data, scan-based customization, and transparent material and part specifications, not a generic one-size-fits-all mask clone.

Local production could matter for cushions, molds, fitting tools, and selected accessories if certified producers can prove materials, biocompatibility, leak performance, cleaning requirements, and quality controls.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open personalized mask fitting network
disruptionConcepts.summary: An open personalized mask fitting network would use face scans, open parametric cushion designs, certified local production, and de-identified fit outcomes to make CPAP masks more custom and less dependent on incumbent SKU portfolios.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The market structure shifts from centralized branded mask families toward local or regional production of validated custom interfaces, with ResMed competing on comfort evidence and service rather than controlling the whole mask catalog.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralized manufacturing and open hardware are central. Bitcoin is not necessary because the trust problem is facial-fit accuracy, medical material quality, production traceability, and clinician acceptance.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Patients or clinics capture face scans, open-design maintainers publish parametric mask interfaces and test protocols, certified producers make cushions or molds, and clinicians or users report leak, comfort, and adherence outcomes.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Verification would depend on signed design versions, scan provenance, material lot traceability, biocompatibility documentation, leak testing, cleaning validation, fit-outcome reporting, and QMSR-aligned production records. Fraud or low-quality output is constrained only if production partners are auditable.
failureModes: A poorly fitted or poorly manufactured mask can worsen leaks, skin irritation, comfort, or therapy adherence.
Small producers may struggle to meet medical-device quality, labeling, liability, and reimbursement requirements.
ResMed's distribution, brand trust, and replacement-supply logistics may outweigh the customization advantage for many patients.
adoptionPath: Start with research, pediatric or craniofacial use cases, and clinician-supervised fitting studies where average-face masks perform poorly.
Expand into certified regional production only after standardized scan workflows, materials, leak testing, and outcome registries are credible.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Open parametric designs and certified local production directly decentralize fitting and selected mask manufacturing while retaining clinical quality constraints.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The technical fitting concept is documented, but a durable network would need clinicians, scan tooling, manufacturers, and quality governance.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Face scanning and 3D-printed or molded custom interfaces are feasible, while medical-grade production, validation, and reimbursement remain difficult.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Custom masks could pressure fit-sensitive segments and accessory margins, but ResMed's catalog, clinical credibility, replacement supply chain, and brand remain powerful.
